So we are working on a project together, daily stand-ups to check in. Got it. But what if we are 6 people, split in different ways across 4 projects. Do we go over all 4 projects in a single stand up? Do we have 4 stand-ups, per project, and only relevant people attend?

Are there any good books/blogs on team/project management for MULTIPLE simultaneous projects on different products with different goals, and a mix and match of team members? A lot of the techniques I know assume a whole team focused on one thing together

The cool thing is that Obsidian sees these links and tags and lets you explore little neighborhoods of your brain once you have tagged things sufficiently https://t.co/FjmUvZkDHB

In obsidian, you can link to other notes you have wiki-style [[like this]] - which lets you build a web of embedded connections between your notes - these show up on my site as shiny little animated links https://t.co/ofCvV9JrKC

right now everyone is doing these "close, but" interactions either thru: slot-machine style re-rolling (go back to the drawing board and come back to me) or through a chat-style interface (you made it red, can I have it blue?) but there has to be something better

This is not a problem that computing power or model size can fix; even expert humans struggle to convey their desires to other expert humans when they have extremely overlapping backgrounds and worldviews; conveying to the robot "close, but" will always be part of the UX
if bug bounties were as common when I was 13 as they are today, my career mighta gone in a very different direction https://t.co/KDiIPKjQhY

Companies that convey the robot as *the solution* to their customers are setting themselves up for resentment by incorrectly framing the power of the tool – companies that allow users to build, monitor, and iterate on boundaries/instructions for the robots quickly will win

If you take this further; the value-add for companies integrating AI into their products is not adding the robot, but knowing when/what to ask the robot, and when to pass up the chain for human intervention, nearly everyone will need to build tooling/UX for this

Right now, the power of the hyper-intelligent robots are as *momentum-builders* - but even the most cutting edge robots can't take you all the way across the finish line. Think of them as extremely powerful firestarters; not firewood
